steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim is that Newland compares his intellect favorably to that of other men in the audience — he must judge his own mind to be superior to theirs.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — '“Newland Archer felt himself distinctly the superior of these chosen specimens of old New York gentility; he had probably read more, thought more, and even seen a good deal more of the world, than any other man of the number.”': Directly states Newland felt intellectually above the other men, which is exactly a favorable self-comparison. ✓
  • Choice B — '“Here was the truth, here was reality, here was the life that belonged to [Newland].”': Concerns Newland's perception of reality, not a comparison of intellects. ✗
  • Choice C — '“Singly [the men around Newland] betrayed their inferiority; but grouped together they represented ‘New York,’ and the habit of masculine solidarity made him accept their doctrine on all the issues called moral.”': Admits the audience had some effect on Newland — the opposite of him feeling superior. ✗
  • Choice D — '“It was Madame Nilsson’s first appearance [in an opera] that winter, and what the daily press had already learned to describe as ‘an exceptionally brilliant audience’ had gathered to hear her.”': Characterizes the other men but makes no claim about Newland's mind relative to theirs. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
